---
title: "Unit 3 Project"
author: "YOUR NAME"
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:  
    keep_md: true
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
    fig_height: 6
    fig_width: 12
    fig_align: 'center'
---

```{r, echo=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

```{r load_libraries, include=FALSE}
# Use this R-Chunk to load all your libraries!
#install.packages("tidyverse") # run this line once in console to get package
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(rootSolve)
```

```{r load_data}
# Use this R-Chunk to import all your datasets!
set.seed(21)
data <- rweibull(2500,0.65,0.8)
data <- data.frame(x=data)
```

## BackGround
To complete the BackGround and Week 1 sections we need data.
For now use this Practice Data.
```{r}
#Clear the workspace.  
#rm(list = ls())
```

> Note: The practice data is 2500 observations from a Weibull distribution with $k=0.65$ and $\lambda=0.8$.  
Thus the true mean is $\mu = 1.09302$ and the true variance is $\sigma^2=3.03839$ ($\sigma = 1.743098$).  
The pdf for the Weibull distribution is $f(x;k,\lambda) = \frac{kx^{k-1}}{\lambda^k}e^{-(\frac{x}{\lambda})^k}$ where $x>0$, $k>0$, and $\lambda >0$.


## Week 1 - The Data
This week we will focus on getting to know the data.  
Read in the data.
```{r}
```

**What is the structure of the data?**  
Check the format and size of the data imported. Is it was you expected. 
```{r}
head(data)
length(data)
dim(data)
```
We see this is a list of 2500 numbers.

**What does the data look like?**  
```{r}
plot(data[,1])
```

> Is there a way to way to create a plot like this in gglot?

What information can we gain from this plot? Truthfully, it is not a pretty figure. But we can see that the values of each the 2500 numbers seem to between 0 and 20 and most of the values are small closer to 0 than to 20.  

This is a sample of a measurement variable. We can model measurement variables (or Stochastic variable or Random variables) with probability models. We write these models mathematically with probability mass functions or probability density functions (and their corresponding cumulative probabilty density functions). So let's use a histogram to visualize probability structure of this sample.
```{r}
hist(data[,1],freq=FALSE,breaks=60)
```
```{r}
ggplot(data,aes(x))+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=x,y=..density..),bins=60)
```

> Include a 1D density contour map.

This histogram confirms our previous observation that all the values are positive and most of the values are small closer to 0. This means the probability that a measurement is close to 0 is much higher than the probability that a measurement is larger than 5. We may also see from this histogram that our data comes from a right skewed distribution. So we may begin to think about what measurement models (or distribution functions) do we know that are right skewed?

**What are the sample statistics?**  
This code calculates the sample mean.
```{r}
mean(data$x)
```
This code calcules the sample standard deviation.
```{r}
sd(data$x)
```
The mean of our sample is 1.051 and the standard deviation is 1.769.  

> Recall: The true mean is 1.09 and the true standard deviation is 1.74.

Can we use this information to predict or infer information about all the possible measurements not just the ones in the sample? How would we go about doing this? What else would we need to know?


## Week 2 - The Model
This week will will focus on getting familiar with the model (or distribution). We will start by exploring the family of Models (or distributions). Then we will determine the parameter values that identify the specific distribution from the family that corresponds to our data.  

Consider the probability density function, $f(x) = \frac{\lambda^r}{\Gamma(r)}x^{r-1}e^{-\lambda x}$ with $x \geq 0$, $r>0$, and $\lambda > 0$. This is a gamma distribution.  
**Check that $f(x)$ could be a probability density function.**  
To do this:
*Plot $f(x)$ and looking to see that $f(x) \geq 0$ in the graph. 
*Calculate $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) dx$ in Mathematica to verify that $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) dx = 1$.

The following code creates a graph of the pdf. Change the left end-point, right end-point, and step size plot the pdf on an appropriate interval.
```{r}
#Calculate values.
#Begin by selecting list of x-values to plug into the pdf.
  #a is the left end-point.
  #b is the right end-point.
  #step is the step size.
a = 0
b = 50
step = 0.1
x <- seq(a,b, step)

#Define the pdf function.
pdf.gamma <- function(x,r=1,lambda=0.5){
(lambda^r/gamma(r))*x^(r-1)*exp(-lambda*x)
}

#Calculate the y-values and create a data frame to use in ggplot()
values <- data.frame(x=x,y=pdf.gamma(x))

#Plot f(x).
ggplot(values,aes(x,y))+
  geom_line()
```
It appears that $f(x)$ is always non-negative for $x \geq 0$.

Calculate $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) dx = 1$ in Mathematica.  
Insert image of Mathematica Calculation: ![](/Users/katjohnson/Documents/Math119/Project3/calc1_pdf.png)

So we are fairly confident at this point that the model we have selected $f(x)$ is really a probability density model.

**Explore the family of distributions.**  
Try different values of parameters and notice how changing the parameters of the model (distribution) changes the look of the density function. The following code creates three graphs of the pdf. Change the left end-point, right end-point, and step size plot the pdf on an appropriate interval. Plot the distribution for at least two choices of the parameters (in addition to the default values).
```{r}
#Calculate values.
#Begin by selecting list of x-values to plug into the pdf.
  #a is the left end-point.
  #b is the right end-point.
  #step is the step size.
a = 0
b = 30
step = 0.1
x <- seq(a,b, step)


#Additional choice of parameters 1.
r1 = 2
lambda1 = 0.5
#Additional choice of parameters 2.
r2 = 2
lambda2 = 0.1
#Calculate the y-values and create a data frame to use in ggplot().
values <- data.frame(x=x,y1=pdf.gamma(x),y2=pdf.gamma(x,r1,lambda1),y3=pdf.gamma(x,r2,lambda2))

#Plot three functions the and compare plots.
ggplot(values,aes(x,y1))+
  geom_line()
ggplot(values,aes(x,y2))+
  geom_line()
ggplot(values,aes(x,y3))+
  geom_line()
```
We see when $r=1$ the peak of the distribution is at the far left. When $r=2$ the peak moves to the right. Changing $\lambda$ stretches the function in the horizontal direction.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the model (distribution) as a function of the parameters.**  
Calculate the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} xf(x) dx$ in Mathematica to find the distribution mean, $\mu$, as a function of the parameters $r$ and $\lambda$.  
Insert image of Mathematica Calculation: ![](/Users/katjohnson/Documents/Math119/Project3/calc2_mean.png)
We see the mean of the distribution is $\mu = \frac{r}{\lambda}$.

Calculate the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x-\mu)^2f(x) dx$ in Mathematica to find the distribution variance, $\sigma^2$, as a function of the parameters $r$ and $\lambda$.  
Insert image of Mathematica Calculation: ![](/Users/katjohnson/Documents/Math119/Project3/calc3_variance.png)
The variance of the distribution is $\sigma^2 = \frac{r}{\lambda^2}$.

**Estimate the values of the parameters for this distribution based on the data.**  
Remember the mean of the sample is 1.051 and the standard deviation of the sample is 1.769. So we need to solve the following system of equations to find the values for $r$ and $\lambda$.Solve
$\begin{align} 1.051 &= \frac{r}{\lambda}\\ (1.769)^2 &= \frac{r}{\lambda^2}\end{align}$.
Using the method of subsitution, we find $\lambda = 0$ or $\lambda = \frac{1.051}{(1.769)^2} = 0.3358513$. Since $\lambda$ must be stictly larger than zero that means $\lambda = 0.3358513$ and $r = 1.051*0.3358513 = 0.3529797$.

>Insert picture of work solving the system.

We have selected and explored a given distribution. We have used the sample data to determine parameter values. What does this tell us? Can we use this information to predict or infer information about all the possible measurements not just the ones in the sample? 

## Week 3 - Use the Model
This week will will focus using the Model we have selected to present conclusions about all possible measurements. Probabilities models contain information about all possible measurements, not just the ones that have been sampled. Models are powerful, but remember we assumed a gamma distribution and then fit the parameters from our sample. The model we have selected is an assumption we have made about the measurements.  
We will begin by simulating data using our model. We will use the built in gamma distribution function in R. Don't forget to look at the documentation in R to make sure you are using the right parameter values. Notice that the gamma distribution in R is programmed with parameters $a = r$ and $s = 1/\lambda$.  
This code creates a sample of 2500 measurements from our model.
```{r}
data.s <- rgamma(2500,0.353,1/0.336)
data.s <- data.frame(x=data.s)
```
Using a histogram we can visualize this sample from our model...
```{r}
hist(data.s[,1],freq=FALSE,breaks=60)
```
```{r}
ggplot(data.s,aes(x))+
  geom_histogram(aes(x=x,y=..density..),bins=60)
```

Using what you know about the definition of the cummulative distribution and integration. Plot the cummulative distribution function.
```{r}
#Calculate values.
#x <- 

fun.cdf1 <- function(x,r=1,lambda=0.5){
  
}
  
#values <- data.frame(x=x,y=)

#Plots.
```

Note: There is a closed form expression of the cdf for this distribution, but it depends on an unfamiliar function. Use Mathematica to calculate the closed form (exact formula) for the cdf.
Insert image of Mathematica Calculation.
Compare your plot of the cdf and a plot of the cdf using the formula.
```{r}
#Calculate values.
#x <- 

fun.cdf2 <- function(x,r=1,lambda=0.5){
}
  
#values <- data.frame(x=x,y=)

#Plots.
```

Calculate each of the following probabilities:
What is the probability that the amount of explosive in a sample will be between _______ and __________? (If you calculated this probability with the pdf, check your answer using the cdf. If you calculated this proability with the cdf, check your answer using the pdf.)
Insert image of Mathematica calculations.

What is the probability that the amount of explosive in a sample will be more than ___________?
(If you calculated this probability with the pdf, check your answer using the cdf. If you calculated this proability with the cdf, check your answer using the pdf.)
Insert image of Mathematica calculations.

What is the 90% percentile of the distribution?
```{r}

```
Check that uniroot() with closed form cdf work in this case.


## Week 4 - A model is an assumption?
Repeat, Observe Visual Fits, and Explore Consequences of Model Assumption
Repeat with Normal, Exponential, and Uniform.
How do the probabilities calculated depend on the assumption made about which model (distribution) to use?
Which do you think is the best model given the data? Explain your reasoning.


## Concluding Thoughts/Summary
